content.problemStatement:
Construct and return a binary tree using two integer arrays, `preorder` and `inorder`. The `preorder` array represents the preorder traversal of the binary tree, while the `inorder` array represents the inorder traversal of the same tree.

**Example:** 

`Preorder` array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] 

`Inorder` array: [2, 1, 4, 3, 5]

The constructed tree




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
The next line contains NNN space separated integers, the preorder array.
The next line contains NNN space separated integers, the inorder array.


Output Format
Complete the given function in the IDE which returns the root of the constructed binary tree.
Output will be `Well Done!` if the tree is constructed correctly, otherwise Runtime errors.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines: an integer N, a line with N space‑separated integers denoting the preorder traversal, and a line with N space‑separated integers denoting the inorder traversal.
content.outputFormat: For each test case print a single line containing the string "Well Done!".

sampleTestCases.output:
Well Done!
Well Done!
Well Done!

solution.approach: The preorder traversal gives the root of the (sub)tree as its first element. Using a hash‑map we store the index of every value in the inorder array. For a given root value we split the inorder array into left and right parts; the sizes of these parts tell us how many elements belong to the left and right subtrees in the preorder array. Recursively build the left subtree, then the right subtree, and link them to the current root. This runs in O(N) time per test case because each node is processed once and all look‑ups are O(1). The recursion depth is at most N, and the auxiliary hash‑map uses O(N) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for the hash‑map and recursion stack.
